summary: This PR adds `FLASK_ENV` support to Flask, introducing `get_env()` and updating `get_debug_flag()` in helpers.py, updating the debug property in app.py, and adding environment-related output and warnings to the CLI. The changes are broadly useful but contain several correctness issues around falsy-string handling, an always-true guard that prints misleading output, and a security concern about FLASK_ENV implicitly enabling the interactive debugger. Test coverage for the new CLI and app-level behaviors is also lacking.

findings.title: `get_debug_flag()` never returns None, so `debug is not None` guard is always True and 'Forcing debug mode' prints unconditionally
findings.detail: The updated `get_debug_flag()` in helpers.py always returns a concrete bool (either `True` or `False`) and can never return `None`. As a result, the guard `if debug is not None:` at line 639 is always `True`, causing `' * Forcing debug mode on/off'` to be printed on every `flask run`, even when neither `FLASK_DEBUG` nor `FLASK_ENV` was explicitly set by the user. This contradicts the intent of the message, which should only appear when the user has *explicitly forced* a debug mode. In production this will misleadingly print 'Forcing debug mode off' on every start. The same always-true condition in `ScriptInfo.load_app` (line 361-362) means `app.debug` is unconditionally overwritten even when the user set nothing, though in practice the values should be consistent. The fix is to either restore the ability of `get_debug_flag()` to return `None` (when neither env var is set), or change the message to one that is always appropriate to print.

findings.title: Falsy-string check (`if not val:`) treats explicit empty-string env vars as unset, masking user intent
findings.detail:
Both `get_env()` (line 59-62) and `get_debug_flag()` (line 66-67) use `if not val:` to fall back to defaults, which conflates `None` (variable not set) with `''` (variable explicitly set to empty string). 

For `get_env()`: `FLASK_ENV=` in a shell is silently overridden to `'production'` rather than being flagged as an unusual/empty value. The test case `('', 'production', False)` in `test_get_env` encodes this arguably-wrong behavior.

For `get_debug_flag()`: `FLASK_DEBUG=` (empty string) falls through to the `get_env()` branch instead of being treated as a non-debug flag. A user who sets `FLASK_DEBUG=` expecting it to mean 'off' will accidentally have debug mode enabled if `FLASK_ENV=development` is also set, which is subtle and dangerous.

The idiomatic fix is to change both guards to `if val is None:` so only a truly absent variable triggers the fallback. Additionally, `''` should be added to the `not-in` exclusion list in `get_debug_flag()` so an empty `FLASK_DEBUG` is treated as falsy (i.e., debug off).

As a style note (from f14), `get_env` could also be simplified to `return os.environ.get('FLASK_ENV') or 'production'`, though this shares the same falsy-string semantics and should only be used after the `is None` fix is applied.

findings.title: Production environment warning, env print line, and updated shell banner lack test coverage
findings.detail:
Three new behaviors in the CLI have no tests:
1. The production-environment warning block (lines 612-617) — no test verifies it is printed when `FLASK_ENV=production` or suppressed when `FLASK_ENV=development`.
2. The `' * Env %s'` print (line 638) — no test verifies the env name appears in run_command output.
3. The shell_command banner was updated from showing `[debug]` to showing `app.env` (line 663) — no test verifies the new banner format.
All three should have corresponding tests in `test_cli.py`.

findings.title: New `make_config()` ENV/DEBUG defaults, debug property getter/setter, and `app.env` ConfigAttribute not tested
findings.detail:
Three related untested behaviors in app.py:
1. The `make_config()` logic (lines 642-645) that calls `get_env()` and `get_debug_flag()` to populate `ENV` and `DEBUG` defaults has no dedicated test. No test asserts that `app.env` and `app.debug` take correct values when `FLASK_ENV`/`FLASK_DEBUG` are or are not set at construction time.
2. The debug attribute was refactored from a `ConfigAttribute` to a property with `_get_debug`, `_set_debug`, and `_set_debug_value`. No tests verify that setting `app.debug` updates both `app.config['DEBUG']` and `app.jinja_env.auto_reload`, or that reading `app.debug` returns `app.config['DEBUG']`.
3. The new `env` ConfigAttribute is not tested — no test checks that `app.env` reads from `app.config['ENV']` or that changing `app.config['ENV']` is reflected in `app.env`.

findings.title: FLASK_ENV implicitly enables the Werkzeug interactive debugger without validation or prominent logging
findings.detail:
Because `get_debug_flag()` returns `True` whenever `get_env() == 'development'`, any process that can inject or mutate `FLASK_ENV` (a `.env` file loaded by python-dotenv, a misconfigured reverse proxy, or a shared-hosting scenario) can silently enable the Werkzeug interactive REPL, which permits arbitrary code execution. The previous model only allowed debug-mode to be enabled via `FLASK_DEBUG`, a single, explicit, documented variable. The new code introduces a second, less-obvious path.

At minimum: (1) the ENV value should be restricted to a known allowlist (`'production'`, `'development'`, `'testing'`) with an error or warning on any other value, and (2) the mapping from `FLASK_ENV=development` → debug-on should be logged prominently so operators are aware. Without validation, a typo in FLASK_ENV also silently falls through to non-debug mode without the operator noticing, which is a related operational risk.

findings.title: Contradictory phrasing: 'and to disable the debug mode' in context that enables it
findings.detail: Line 133 reads: "To enable all development features (and to disable the debug mode)". This is contradictory — setting `FLASK_ENV=development` *enables* debug mode, not disables it. This parenthetical will confuse readers and should be corrected or removed.

findings.title: Dead branch in `test_get_debug_flag`: `assert get_debug_flag() is None` can never be reached
findings.detail: After updating the parametrize table, `expected_flag` is never `None` in any test case (all cases now use `False` or `True`). The branch `if expected_flag is None: assert get_debug_flag() is None` (lines 897-898) is therefore dead code and can never execute. This means the test no longer documents or verifies any scenario where `get_debug_flag()` returns `None`. If the `None`-return path has been intentionally removed from `get_debug_flag()`, the dead branch should be deleted; if it can still be reached, a test case should cover it. This is reported by both the correctness and style reviewers.

findings.title: `test_get_env` does not isolate `FLASK_DEBUG`, potentially polluting `get_debug_flag()` assertions
findings.detail: `test_get_env` patches `FLASK_ENV` but never clears `FLASK_DEBUG`. If `FLASK_DEBUG` is set in the test-runner environment (e.g. `FLASK_DEBUG=1`), `get_debug_flag()` will return `True` for every parametrize case regardless of `FLASK_ENV`, causing `assert get_debug_flag() == debug` to fail or silently pass for wrong reasons. The test should add `monkeypatch.delenv('FLASK_DEBUG', raising=False)` to guarantee isolation.

findings.title: Shell code block missing `.. code-block::` directive, will render incorrectly in Sphinx
findings.detail: The code example at lines 67-68 appears as literal paragraph text rather than inside a `.. code-block::` or `::` block with four-space indentation. The surrounding documentation consistently uses indented code blocks for shell examples. Without the proper directive, Sphinx will not render this as a code block, making the docs harder to read.

findings.title: Production warning only fires for the exact string `'production'`, silently skipped for non-development environments
findings.detail: The condition `if get_env() == 'production'` only warns when the env is exactly `'production'`. Users with `FLASK_ENV=staging`, `FLASK_ENV=test`, or a typo like `FLASK_ENV=productiion` get no warning even though running the development server in those environments is equally inappropriate. Furthermore, because `FLASK_ENV` is not validated against an allowlist, setting it to any arbitrary value (accidentally or maliciously) fully suppresses the warning. The intent described in the PR and docs is to warn whenever the user is *not* in development mode, so the guard should be `if get_env() != 'development':` (or enumerate a set of safe envs). The warning is also emitted to stdout rather than stderr, making it easy to miss in CI/CD pipelines.

findings.title: Production warning uses `click.secho` while surrounding output uses plain `print`, mixing output styles
findings.detail: The run-command output block (lines 637-640) consistently uses `print(...)`. The new production-environment warning switches to `click.secho(...)`, mixing two output styles in the same function. Either convert to `print` for consistency, or document a deliberate reason for the difference (e.g., to allow color output for the warning).

findings.title: `_set_debug_value` triggers early Jinja environment creation before config files are loaded
findings.detail: In `_set_debug_value` (line 816), accessing `self.jinja_env.auto_reload` triggers the `locked_cached_property` to build and permanently cache the Jinja2 environment. If `_set_debug_value` is called early (e.g. from `ScriptInfo.load_app` via `app.debug = debug` before `app.config.from_*` runs), the Jinja env is created and cached with whatever `templates_auto_reload` evaluates to at that moment. Any subsequent change to `TEMPLATES_AUTO_RELOAD` or `DEBUG` via a config file will not affect the already-cached `jinja_env.auto_reload`. The deleted `_reconfigure_for_run_debug` docstring explicitly acknowledged this ordering concern; the new code is subject to the same problem but without any documentation warning.

findings.title: `get_debug_flag()` interaction when both `FLASK_ENV=development` and `FLASK_DEBUG=0` are set is not tested
findings.detail: When `FLASK_DEBUG` is explicitly set, `get_debug_flag()` returns based on that value and ignores `FLASK_ENV`. However, there is no test covering the scenario where both `FLASK_ENV=development` and `FLASK_DEBUG=0` are set simultaneously. This scenario is important to confirm that an explicit `FLASK_DEBUG=0` correctly overrides the `FLASK_ENV=development` implied-debug behavior.

findings.title: Grammar: 'On older Flask version' should be 'On older Flask versions'
findings.detail: Line 35 reads "On older Flask version (before 1.0)" — `version` should be plural: `versions`.

findings.title: Awkward grammar: 'The other default environment which is known is'
findings.detail: The sentence "The other default environment which is known is `development`." is grammatically awkward. Consider: "The other recognised environment is `development`."

findings.title: Missing blank line between `_get_debug` and `_set_debug` method definitions
findings.detail: The `_get_debug` and `_set_debug` methods are defined back-to-back without a blank line between them. The surrounding code in the file consistently separates method definitions with a blank line. Add one blank line between the two defs to match the existing convention.

findings.title: `env` ConfigAttribute placed in the middle of the class body rather than near the other class-level attributes
findings.detail: The `env` `ConfigAttribute` and its `#:` docblock are placed in the middle of where the old `_reconfigure_for_run_debug` method was. The existing style places class-level `ConfigAttribute` declarations together near the top of the class (alongside `testing`, etc.). Moving `env` to that section would be more consistent with the rest of the class layout.

findings.title: Env print label is unusually terse compared to sibling lines
findings.detail: Line 638 prints `' * Env %s'` while adjacent lines use more descriptive labels like `' * Serving Flask app "%s"'` and `' * Forcing debug mode %s'`. Consider using `'Environment: %s'` or `'Env: %s'` (with a colon) for readability and consistency with server startup output style.

findings.title: `get_debug_flag()` always returns a bool, so `debug is not None` guard is always True and 'Forcing debug mode' prints unconditionally
Production warning only fires for the exact string `'production'`, silently skipped for other non-development environments
findings.detail: The condition `if get_env() == 'production'` only warns when the env is exactly the string `'production'`. If a user sets `FLASK_ENV=staging`, `FLASK_ENV=test`, or even mis-spells it as `FLASK_ENV=productiion`, no warning is shown even though the development server is equally inappropriate. Conversely the intent in the PR description and docs is clearly to warn whenever the user is *not* in development mode, so the guard should likely be `if get_env() != 'development'`.

findings.title: `_set_debug_value` triggers early Jinja environment creation, caching it before config files are loaded
findings.detail:
In `_set_debug_value` (line 816), accessing `self.jinja_env.auto_reload` triggers the `locked_cached_property` to build and permanently cache the Jinja2 environment. If `_set_debug_value` is called early (e.g. from `ScriptInfo.load_app` 
 via `app.debug = debug` before `app.config.from_*` runs), the Jinja env is created and cached with whatever `templates_auto_reload` evaluates to at that moment. Any subsequent change to `TEMPLATES_AUTO_RELOAD` or `DEBUG` via a config file will not affect the already-cached `jinja_env.auto_reload`. The deleted `_reconfigure_for_run_debug` docstring explicitly acknowledged this ordering concern; the new code is subject to the same problem without any warning.

findings.title: `get_env()` treats empty string `FLASK_ENV` as unset, silently masking an explicit user mistake
findings.detail: The guard `if not val:` catches both the case where `FLASK_ENV` is absent (`os.environ.get` returns `None`) and the case where it is explicitly set to the empty string (`''`). In the `test_get_env` test, the parametrize case `('', 'production', False)` calls `monkeypatch.setenv('FLASK_ENV', '')`, which sets the variable to an empty string — a value that is silently overridden to `'production'`. This is the same `!val` falsy-check antipattern: `FLASK_ENV=` (empty) in a real shell environment is a valid (if unusual) configuration, but here it is indistinguishable from absence. If the intent is to produce `'production'` only when the variable is truly absent, the check should be `if val is None:` rather than `if not val:`.

findings.title: `get_debug_flag()` treats empty string `FLASK_DEBUG` as unset, bypassing the explicit value
findings.detail: The guard `if not val:` on line 67 causes an explicitly set `FLASK_DEBUG=` (empty string, e.g. `FLASK_DEBUG= flask run`) to fall through to the `get_env()` branch instead of being treated as a truthy/falsy debug setting. The previous code had the same issue with the empty-string case, but the test at line 889 now encodes `('', False, False)` as the expected result for `FLASK_DEBUG=''`, meaning any user who deliberately sets `FLASK_DEBUG=` expecting it to be falsy will accidentally have debug mode enabled if `FLASK_ENV=development` is also set. This is particularly subtle because on many shells `FLASK_DEBUG=` is syntactically valid and would be expected to disable debug mode (consistent with the `'0'`, `'false'`, `'no'` treatment), yet here it instead defers to FLASK_ENV. The fix is to change the guard to `if val is None:` so that an explicitly empty `FLASK_DEBUG` is treated as a non-debug flag (its `.lower()` would not be in the exclusion list so it would return `True` — which is also wrong; the better fix is `if val is None: …` and adding `''` to the falsy set in the `not-in` check).

findings.title: Dead code: `if expected_flag is None` branch can never be reached
findings.detail: After the parametrize change, `expected_flag` is never `None` in any test case (it is always `False`, `False`, `False`, `False`, or `True`). The branch `if expected_flag is None: assert get_debug_flag() is None` on line 897-898 can therefore never execute. While this is a test-correctness issue rather than a production bug, it means the test no longer exercises the `None`-return path of `get_debug_flag`, which was previously reachable (old signature had a `default` parameter). If the `None` path is intentionally removed from `get_debug_flag`, the dead branch should be removed too; if it is still reachable in some other way, a test case should cover it.

findings.title: `test_get_env` does not isolate `FLASK_DEBUG`, so its `get_debug_flag()` assertions may be polluted by a pre-existing `FLASK_DEBUG` env var
findings.detail: `test_get_env` uses `monkeypatch.setenv('FLASK_ENV', env)` but never clears or patches `FLASK_DEBUG`. If `FLASK_DEBUG` happens to be set in the test-runner environment (e.g. `FLASK_DEBUG=1`), `get_debug_flag()` will return `True` for every parametrize case regardless of `FLASK_ENV`, causing the `assert get_debug_flag() == debug` assertions to be wrong. The test should also call `monkeypatch.delenv('FLASK_DEBUG', raising=False)` to guarantee isolation.

findings.title: FLASK_ENV controls debug mode — untrusted env var can silently enable the Werkzeug interactive debugger
findings.detail: get_env() reads FLASK_ENV from the process environment without any validation. get_debug_flag() then implicitly sets debug=True whenever FLASK_ENV=='development' (helpers.py:68-70). In turn, app.debug drives use_debugger in Flask.run() (app.py:896). Any process that can inject or mutate FLASK_ENV (e.g. a .env file loaded by python-dotenv, a misconfigured reverse proxy that passes the env var, or a shared-hosting scenario) can therefore silently enable the Werkzeug interactive REPL, which allows arbitrary code execution. The previous model only allowed debug to be enabled by FLASK_DEBUG, which was a single, explicit, documented variable. Now a second env-var path exists that is even less obvious because the connection between FLASK_ENV='development' and debug-mode-on is indirect and not validated. At minimum the mapping from FLASK_ENV to debug should be logged prominently; ideally the ENV value should be restricted to a known allowlist ('production', 'development', 'testing') with an error or warning on any other value, so that a typo does not silently fall through to non-debug mode without the operator noticing.

findings.title: No allowlist validation on FLASK_ENV value — arbitrary string stored in app config and printed to users
findings.detail: get_env() accepts any string from the environment and returns it verbatim (defaulting to 'production' only on empty/unset). This value is stored as app.config['ENV'] and is printed to stdout in cli.py line 638 (' * Env %s' % get_env()) and in the shell banner (cli.py line 663). While neither location constitutes a direct injection vector in isolation, the value flows into user-visible output with no sanitisation. A validation step (e.g., asserting the value is one of a fixed set such as 'production', 'development', 'testing') would eliminate any future risk of unexpected values being surfaced or misinterpreted.

findings.title: Production-environment warning can be suppressed silently by setting FLASK_ENV to any non-'production' string
findings.detail: The production warning in run_command is only issued when get_env() == 'production'. Because get_env() trusts the FLASK_ENV environment variable without validation, a user who accidentally or maliciously sets FLASK_ENV to any arbitrary value (e.g. 'prod', 'staging', '') will bypass the warning entirely. The warning message is also not elevated to a hard error or stderr, making it easy to miss in CI/CD log streams. This is a defense-in-depth concern: the check should either enumerate the non-production values that suppress the warning, or issue the warning whenever the environment is not explicitly 'development' or 'testing'.

findings.title: Missing blank line between two method definitions inside class body
findings.detail: `_get_debug` and `_set_debug` are defined back-to-back without a blank line between them. The surrounding code in the file consistently separates method definitions with a blank line. Add one blank line between the two defs to match that convention.

findings.title: Class-level attribute docstring placed after deleted method block is oddly indented / misplaced
findings.detail: The `env` `ConfigAttribute` and its preceding `#:` docblock appear in the middle of what was the old `_reconfigure_for_run_debug` method's location. The existing style places class-level `ConfigAttribute` declarations together near the top of the class (around the other `#:` docblocks for `testing`, etc.). Moving `env` up there would be more consistent with the rest of the class layout.

findings.title: Production warning uses `click.secho` while surrounding output uses plain `print`
findings.detail: The run-command output (lines 637-640) consistently uses `print(...)`. The new warning block switches to `click.secho`, mixing two output styles in the same function. Either convert everything in the block to `print` (keeping consistency with the surrounding code) or document a deliberate reason for the difference.

findings.title: Env print line lacks a colon separator, inconsistent with sibling lines
findings.detail: Line 638 prints `' * Env %s'` while the adjacent lines print `' * Serving Flask app "%s"'` and `' * Forcing debug mode %s'`. The pattern elsewhere in the block uses a descriptive label followed by the value with no extra separator, but the label `Env` is unusually terse compared to the others. Consider `'Environment: %s'` or `'Env: %s'` with a colon for readability and to match the style of the server startup output.

findings.title: `get_env` uses a two-branch if/assign instead of a one-liner with `or`
findings.detail:
The old `get_debug_flag` used a similar pattern, but the idiomatic Python style visible in this file and in `get_debug_flag` itself is a concise conditional. The three lines:
```python
val = os.environ.get('FLASK_ENV')
if not val:
    val = 'production'
return val
```
can be written as:
```python
return os.environ.get('FLASK_ENV') or 'production'
```
This is shorter, consistent with Python conventions, and easier to scan.

findings.title: Dead branch: `assert get_debug_flag() is None` can never be reached after removing the `default` parameter
findings.detail: Line 897-898 still contains `assert get_debug_flag() is None` guarded by `if debug == ''`. However, `get_debug_flag()` no longer returns `None` (it now returns `False` when `FLASK_ENV` is not `development`). The parametrize table no longer includes a case where `expected_flag` is `None`, so this branch is dead code. It should be removed or updated to match the new behaviour.

findings.title: Parenthetical remark "and to disable the debug mode" is confusing / contradictory
findings.detail: Line 133 reads: "To enable all development features (and to disable the debug mode)". This is contradictory — setting `FLASK_ENV=development` *enables* debug mode, not disables it. The existing surrounding prose is clear and precise; this phrase will confuse readers.

findings.title: Shell code block missing leading `$` prompt and indentation, inconsistent with surrounding examples
findings.detail:
The code example at lines 67-68:
```
$ export FLASK_ENV=development
$ flask run
```
is not indented inside a `.. code-block::` or `::` block — it appears as literal paragraph text. The surrounding documentation consistently indents shell examples with four spaces inside a `::` block (see the "Application Discovery" section). The missing indentation will render incorrectly in Sphinx.

findings.title: Awkward phrasing "The other default environment which is known is"
findings.detail: The sentence "The other default environment which is known is `development`." is grammatically awkward. The existing docs use clear, direct prose. Consider: "The other recognised environment is `development`."

findings.title: Grammar: "On older Flask version" should be "On older Flask versions"
findings.detail: Line 35 reads "On older Flask version (before 1.0)" — `version` should be plural (`versions`). The surrounding documentation is carefully proofread; this is a minor typo.

findings.title: make_config() ENV/DEBUG defaults not directly tested
findings.detail: The new make_config() logic (lines 642-645) that calls get_env() and get_debug_flag() to populate 'ENV' and 'DEBUG' defaults on the Flask app has no dedicated test. There is no test asserting that app.env and app.debug take the correct values when FLASK_ENV/FLASK_DEBUG are or are not set during app construction.

findings.title: New debug property getter/setter and _set_debug_value() not tested
findings.detail: The debug attribute was refactored from a ConfigAttribute to a property with _get_debug, _set_debug, and _set_debug_value (which also sets jinja_env.auto_reload). There are no tests verifying that setting app.debug updates both app.config['DEBUG'] and app.jinja_env.auto_reload, nor that reading app.debug returns app.config['DEBUG'] correctly.

findings.title: New app.env ConfigAttribute not tested
findings.detail: The new 'env' ConfigAttribute is not directly tested — no test checks that app.env reads from app.config['ENV'] or that changing app.config['ENV'] is reflected in app.env.

findings.title: Production environment warning in run_command not tested
findings.detail: The new production-environment warning (lines 612-617) that calls click.secho when get_env() == 'production' has no test coverage. There is no test in test_cli.py that verifies this warning is printed (or not printed) based on FLASK_ENV.

findings.title: 'Env <env>' print in run_command not tested
findings.detail: The new 'print(' * Env %s' % get_env())' line (line 638) has no test verifying that the env name is printed in the run_command output.

findings.title: Shell banner now uses app.env instead of debug flag — not tested
findings.detail: The shell_command banner format changed from showing '[debug]' to showing app.env (line 663). There is no test in test_cli.py that verifies the new banner format includes the environment name.

findings.title: get_debug_flag() interaction with FLASK_ENV when FLASK_DEBUG is explicitly set is not tested
findings.detail: When FLASK_DEBUG is explicitly set (e.g. to '1'), get_debug_flag() returns based on the FLASK_DEBUG value and ignores FLASK_ENV. There is no test covering the scenario where both FLASK_ENV=development and FLASK_DEBUG=0 are set simultaneously (i.e., FLASK_DEBUG should override FLASK_ENV's implied debug value).

findings.title: test_get_debug_flag no longer tests get_debug_flag() returning None
findings.detail: The original test included a case ('', None, True) where get_debug_flag() was expected to return None when FLASK_DEBUG was empty and no default was given. The updated parametrize list (line 888) changes this to ('', False, False), removing coverage of any scenario where get_debug_flag() could return None. The code path where get_debug_flag() is called with no FLASK_ENV set and no FLASK_DEBUG set and returns False is tested, but the None branch inside test_get_debug_flag (lines 897-898) is now dead code since None is never expected.

findings.title: `get_debug_flag()` never returns None, so `debug is not None` guard is always True and 'Forcing debug mode' prints unconditionally
findings.detail: The updated `get_debug_flag()` in helpers.py always returns a concrete bool (either `True` or `False`) and can never return `None`. As a result, the guard `if debug is not None:` at line 639 is always `True`, causing `' * Forcing debug mode on/off'` to be printed on every `flask run`, even when neither `FLASK_DEBUG` nor `FLASK_ENV` was explicitly set by the user. This contradicts the intent of the message, which should only appear when the user has *explicitly forced* a debug mode. In production this will misleadingly print 'Forcing debug mode off' on every start. The same always-true condition in `ScriptInfo.load_app` (line 361-362) means `app.debug` is unconditionally overwritten even when the user set nothing, though in practice the values should be consistent. The fix is to either restore the ability of `get_debug_flag()` to return `None` (when neither env var is set), or change the message to one that is always appropriate to print.

findings.title: Falsy-string check (`if not val:`) treats explicit empty-string env vars as unset, masking user intent
findings.detail:
Both `get_env()` (line 59-62) and `get_debug_flag()` (line 66-67) use `if not val:` to fall back to defaults, which conflates `None` (variable not set) with `''` (variable explicitly set to empty string). 

For `get_env()`: `FLASK_ENV=` in a shell is silently overridden to `'production'` rather than being flagged as an unusual/empty value. The test case `('', 'production', False)` in `test_get_env` encodes this arguably-wrong behavior.

For `get_debug_flag()`: `FLASK_DEBUG=` (empty string) falls through to the `get_env()` branch instead of being treated as a non-debug flag. A user who sets `FLASK_DEBUG=` expecting it to mean 'off' will accidentally have debug mode enabled if `FLASK_ENV=development` is also set, which is subtle and dangerous.

The idiomatic fix is to change both guards to `if val is None:` so only a truly absent variable triggers the fallback. Additionally, `''` should be added to the `not-in` exclusion list in `get_debug_flag()` so an empty `FLASK_DEBUG` is treated as falsy (i.e., debug off).

As a style note (from f14), `get_env` could also be simplified to `return os.environ.get('FLASK_ENV') or 'production'`, though this shares the same falsy-string semantics and should only be used after the `is None` fix is applied.

findings.title: Production environment warning, env print line, and updated shell banner lack test coverage
findings.detail:
Three new behaviors in the CLI have no tests:
1. The production-environment warning block (lines 612-617) — no test verifies it is printed when `FLASK_ENV=production` or suppressed when `FLASK_ENV=development`.
2. The `' * Env %s'` print (line 638) — no test verifies the env name appears in run_command output.
3. The shell_command banner was updated from showing `[debug]` to showing `app.env` (line 663) — no test verifies the new banner format.
All three should have corresponding tests in `test_cli.py`.

findings.title: New `make_config()` ENV/DEBUG defaults, debug property getter/setter, and `app.env` ConfigAttribute not tested
findings.detail:
Three related untested behaviors in app.py:
1. The `make_config()` logic (lines 642-645) that calls `get_env()` and `get_debug_flag()` to populate `ENV` and `DEBUG` defaults has no dedicated test. No test asserts that `app.env` and `app.debug` take correct values when `FLASK_ENV`/`FLASK_DEBUG` are or are not set at construction time.
2. The debug attribute was refactored from a `ConfigAttribute` to a property with `_get_debug`, `_set_debug`, and `_set_debug_value`. No tests verify that setting `app.debug` updates both `app.config['DEBUG']` and `app.jinja_env.auto_reload`, or that reading `app.debug` returns `app.config['DEBUG']`.
3. The new `env` ConfigAttribute is not tested — no test checks that `app.env` reads from `app.config['ENV']` or that changing `app.config['ENV']` is reflected in `app.env`.

findings.title: FLASK_ENV implicitly enables the Werkzeug interactive debugger without validation or prominent logging
findings.detail:
Because `get_debug_flag()` returns `True` whenever `get_env() == 'development'`, any process that can inject or mutate `FLASK_ENV` (a `.env` file loaded by python-dotenv, a misconfigured reverse proxy, or a shared-hosting scenario) can silently enable the Werkzeug interactive REPL, which permits arbitrary code execution. The previous model only allowed debug-mode to be enabled via `FLASK_DEBUG`, a single, explicit, documented variable. The new code introduces a second, less-obvious path.

At minimum: (1) the ENV value should be restricted to a known allowlist (`'production'`, `'development'`, `'testing'`) with an error or warning on any other value, and (2) the mapping from `FLASK_ENV=development` → debug-on should be logged prominently so operators are aware. Without validation, a typo in FLASK_ENV also silently falls through to non-debug mode without the operator noticing, which is a related operational risk.

findings.title: Contradictory phrasing: 'and to disable the debug mode' in context that enables it
findings.detail: Line 133 reads: "To enable all development features (and to disable the debug mode)". This is contradictory — setting `FLASK_ENV=development` *enables* debug mode, not disables it. This parenthetical will confuse readers and should be corrected or removed.

findings.title: Dead branch in `test_get_debug_flag`: `assert get_debug_flag() is None` can never be reached
findings.detail: After updating the parametrize table, `expected_flag` is never `None` in any test case (all cases now use `False` or `True`). The branch `if expected_flag is None: assert get_debug_flag() is None` (lines 897-898) is therefore dead code and can never execute. This means the test no longer documents or verifies any scenario where `get_debug_flag()` returns `None`. If the `None`-return path has been intentionally removed from `get_debug_flag()`, the dead branch should be deleted; if it can still be reached, a test case should cover it. This is reported by both the correctness and style reviewers.

findings.title: `test_get_env` does not isolate `FLASK_DEBUG`, potentially polluting `get_debug_flag()` assertions
findings.detail: `test_get_env` patches `FLASK_ENV` but never clears `FLASK_DEBUG`. If `FLASK_DEBUG` is set in the test-runner environment (e.g. `FLASK_DEBUG=1`), `get_debug_flag()` will return `True` for every parametrize case regardless of `FLASK_ENV`, causing `assert get_debug_flag() == debug` to fail or silently pass for wrong reasons. The test should add `monkeypatch.delenv('FLASK_DEBUG', raising=False)` to guarantee isolation.

findings.title: Shell code block missing `.. code-block::` directive, will render incorrectly in Sphinx
findings.detail: The code example at lines 67-68 appears as literal paragraph text rather than inside a `.. code-block::` or `::` block with four-space indentation. The surrounding documentation consistently uses indented code blocks for shell examples. Without the proper directive, Sphinx will not render this as a code block, making the docs harder to read.

findings.title: Production warning only fires for the exact string `'production'`, silently skipped for non-development environments
findings.detail: The condition `if get_env() == 'production'` only warns when the env is exactly `'production'`. Users with `FLASK_ENV=staging`, `FLASK_ENV=test`, or a typo like `FLASK_ENV=productiion` get no warning even though running the development server in those environments is equally inappropriate. Furthermore, because `FLASK_ENV` is not validated against an allowlist, setting it to any arbitrary value (accidentally or maliciously) fully suppresses the warning. The intent described in the PR and docs is to warn whenever the user is *not* in development mode, so the guard should be `if get_env() != 'development':` (or enumerate a set of safe envs). The warning is also emitted to stdout rather than stderr, making it easy to miss in CI/CD pipelines.

findings.title: Production warning uses `click.secho` while surrounding output uses plain `print`, mixing output styles
findings.detail: The run-command output block (lines 637-640) consistently uses `print(...)`. The new production-environment warning switches to `click.secho(...)`, mixing two output styles in the same function. Either convert to `print` for consistency, or document a deliberate reason for the difference (e.g., to allow color output for the warning).

findings.title: `_set_debug_value` triggers early Jinja environment creation before config files are loaded
findings.detail: In `_set_debug_value` (line 816), accessing `self.jinja_env.auto_reload` triggers the `locked_cached_property` to build and permanently cache the Jinja2 environment. If `_set_debug_value` is called early (e.g. from `ScriptInfo.load_app` via `app.debug = debug` before `app.config.from_*` runs), the Jinja env is created and cached with whatever `templates_auto_reload` evaluates to at that moment. Any subsequent change to `TEMPLATES_AUTO_RELOAD` or `DEBUG` via a config file will not affect the already-cached `jinja_env.auto_reload`. The deleted `_reconfigure_for_run_debug` docstring explicitly acknowledged this ordering concern; the new code is subject to the same problem but without any documentation warning.

findings.title: `get_debug_flag()` interaction when both `FLASK_ENV=development` and `FLASK_DEBUG=0` are set is not tested
findings.detail: When `FLASK_DEBUG` is explicitly set, `get_debug_flag()` returns based on that value and ignores `FLASK_ENV`. However, there is no test covering the scenario where both `FLASK_ENV=development` and `FLASK_DEBUG=0` are set simultaneously. This scenario is important to confirm that an explicit `FLASK_DEBUG=0` correctly overrides the `FLASK_ENV=development` implied-debug behavior.

findings.title: Grammar: 'On older Flask version' should be 'On older Flask versions'
findings.detail: Line 35 reads "On older Flask version (before 1.0)" — `version` should be plural: `versions`.

findings.title: Awkward grammar: 'The other default environment which is known is'
findings.detail: The sentence "The other default environment which is known is `development`." is grammatically awkward. Consider: "The other recognised environment is `development`."

findings.title: Missing blank line between `_get_debug` and `_set_debug` method definitions
findings.detail: The `_get_debug` and `_set_debug` methods are defined back-to-back without a blank line between them. The surrounding code in the file consistently separates method definitions with a blank line. Add one blank line between the two defs to match the existing convention.

findings.title: `env` ConfigAttribute placed in the middle of the class body rather than near the other class-level attributes
findings.detail: The `env` `ConfigAttribute` and its `#:` docblock are placed in the middle of where the old `_reconfigure_for_run_debug` method was. The existing style places class-level `ConfigAttribute` declarations together near the top of the class (alongside `testing`, etc.). Moving `env` to that section would be more consistent with the rest of the class layout.

findings.title: Env print label is unusually terse compared to sibling lines
findings.detail: Line 638 prints `' * Env %s'` while adjacent lines use more descriptive labels like `' * Serving Flask app "%s"'` and `' * Forcing debug mode %s'`. Consider using `'Environment: %s'` or `'Env: %s'` (with a colon) for readability and consistency with server startup output style.
